title: 2 3 Gradient and directional derivative
body:
2.3 GRADIENT AND DIRECTIONAL DERIVATIVE

Given a scalar field Ï†(x, y, z), the gradient of Ï† is a vector field given by

âˆ‚Ï† âˆ‚Ï† âˆ‚Ï† âˆ‡Ï† = grad Ï† = i+ j+ k. grad : scalar â†’ vector âˆ‚x âˆ‚y âˆ‚z

The gradient of Ï† at the point P , âˆ‡Ï†|P , is a vector pointing in the direction of maximum increase with the rate of increase given by the magnitude of âˆ‡Ï†|P . The direction and rate of maximum decrease is given by âˆ’âˆ‡Ï†|P .

The rate of change of Ï† at the point P in the direction of the vector b is given by the directional derivative âˆ‚Ï† âˆ‚Ï† âˆ‚Ï† (âˆ‡Ï†|P ) Â· bÌ‚ = b1 + b2 + b3 âˆ‚x P âˆ‚y P âˆ‚z P

where bÌ‚ = b1 i + b2 j + b3 k is the unit vector in the direction of b.

Given a scalar field, such as the temperature at every point in the room, we would like to be able to measure how the scalar field changes with position. This information is given by the gradient of the scalar field. At a point P , the gradient tells you the direction and magnitude of maximum increase of the scalar field. If we want to know the rate of change of Ï† in a particular direction, we use the directional derivative.

Notice that gradient takes a scalar field and gives back a vector field â€” for every point in space, it gives a vector pointing in the direction that Ï† is increasing fastest, with the rate of increase given by the magnitude of the vector. We will return to the subject of vector fields in more detail in the next topic.

Example 1 Let Ï†(x, y, z) = x2 y 3 z + 2y be a scalar field. Calculate grad(Ï†).

ï£« ï£¶ 2xy 3 z F 2xy 3 zi + (3x2 y 2 z + 2)j + x2 y 3 k = ï£­3x2 y 2 z + 2ï£¸ F x2 y 3

The directional derivative of Ï† at the point P in the direction of the unit vector bÌ‚ is given by the dot product of bÌ‚ with âˆ‡Ï†|P . Recall that the dot product of two vectors is

u = u1 i + u2 j + u3 k, v = v1 i + v2 j + v3 k, u Â· v = u1 v1 + u2 v2 + u3 v3 .

It is important to make sure that the direction vector bÌ‚ is a unit vector!

Example 2 Calculate the directional derivative of the temperature field Ï† = x2 yz in the direction i + 2j + 2k at the point P (âˆ’1, 1, 3).

F 3 F

This means that if you start at the point P (âˆ’1, 1, 3) and move in the direction i + 2j + 2k then the instantaneous rate of change of temperature is equal to 32 .

Notice that the directional derivative just gives us back a number : the rate of change of Ï† at a point P in the direction b.

We can now prove that the directional derivative does indeed give us the rate of change of a scalar field Ï†(x, y, z) in the direction bÌ‚. To do this, we will first remind ourselves how to define a straight line path in three dimensions.

Example 3 Find a parametric vector equation for the line passing through the points P (3, 1, 2) and Q(4, 4, 3).

ï£« ï£¶ ï£« ï£¶ x(t) 3+t F ï£­y(t)ï£¸ = ï£­1 + 3tï£¸ F z(t) 2+t

Proof of formula: We will prove the result in three dimensions. The argument in other dimensions is similar.

We want to calculate the rate of change of Ï† in the direction of the unit vector bÌ‚, given by ï£« ï£¶ b1 bÌ‚ = ï£­ b2 ï£¸ . b3 First we define the straight-line path from a point P (x0 , y0 , z0 ) in the direction of bÌ‚, ï£« ï£¶ ï£« ï£¶ ï£« ï£¶ ï£« ï£¶ x(s) x0 b1 b1 s ï£­ y(s) ï£¸ = ï£­ y0 ï£¸ + ï£­ b2 ï£¸ s = ï£­ b2 s ï£¸ , z(s) z0 b3 b3 s

where s is the distance from the point P .

Now, Ï†(x, y, x) depends upon x, y and z which in turn depend upon s. Hence, via the chain rule: ï£« âˆ‚Ï† ï£¶ ï£¬ âˆ‚x ï£· ï£¬ ï£· ï£« ï£¶ ï£¬ ï£· ï£¬ âˆ‚Ï† ï£· b 1 dÏ† âˆ‚Ï† âˆ‚x âˆ‚Ï† âˆ‚y âˆ‚Ï† âˆ‚z âˆ‚Ï† âˆ‚Ï† âˆ‚Ï† ï£¬ âˆ‚y ï£· Â· = + + = b1 + b2 + b3 = ï£¬ ï£· ï£­ b2 ï£¸ = (âˆ‡Ï†) Â· bÌ‚ ds âˆ‚x âˆ‚s âˆ‚y âˆ‚s âˆ‚z âˆ‚s âˆ‚x âˆ‚y âˆ‚z ï£¬ ï£¬ ï£· ï£· b3 âˆ‚Ï† ï£­ ï£¸ âˆ‚z as required. F

With the directional derivative we can calculate the rate of change of a scalar field in any direction. But which direction should we move in to increase the scalar field as quickly as possible? In other words, which direction b gives the maximum directional derivative?

In first year you learned that the dot product of u and v could be written as

u Â· v = |u||v| cos Î¸,

where Î¸ is the angle between the two vectors. So the directional derivative is

(âˆ‡Ï†|P ) Â· bÌ‚ = |âˆ‡Ï†|P ||bÌ‚| cos Î¸,

where Î¸ is now the angle between |âˆ‡Ï†|P | and bÌ‚. But the gradient is fixed and |bÌ‚| = 1 because bÌ‚ is a unit vector. So the maximum value of the directional derivative will occur when cos Î¸ = 1, that is, when Î¸ = 0 and âˆ‡Ï†|P and bÌ‚ point in the same direction.

So the direction that maximises the directional derivative is just the unit vector pointing in the same direction as âˆ‡Ï†|P . Whatâ€™s more, the magnitude of the maximum rate of change is given by |âˆ‡Ï†|P |. This means that the gradient of a scalar field at the point P can be understood as a vector pointing in the direction of maximum increase of Ï† and with magnitude equal to the rate of maximum increase. Likewise, the direction and magnitude of maximum decrease of Ï† at the point P is given by âˆ’âˆ‡Ï†|P .

Example 4 Consider the scalar field Ï†(x, y, z) = x2 z+2y 2 âˆ’yez . What is the magnitude and direction of the maximum rate of change of Ï† at P (1, 2, 0)?
